text.title: AI Feature UX Pattern Library Core Rules
text.summary: Four testable rules every application of AI Feature UX Pattern Library enforces. These are HARD rules — user-supplied input cannot override them.

rule.statement: AI Feature UX Pattern Library gate criteria are unambiguous and pre-registered. The gate either passes or names the missing criterion.

rationale.source: Stage-Gate process / Robert Cooper, Winning at New Products (2017)

rule.rationale: Soft criteria become political. Hard criteria route the conversation to evidence.

rule.statement: Kill-criteria are written DURING the brief, not the gate review. A feature with no kill-criteria has no exit ramp.
rule.rationale: Brief-time kill criteria are honest. Gate-time ones are rationalized.

rule.statement: Cross-functional consumers (support, finance, legal, comms) are pre-briefed and have written sign-off before gate review.
rule.rationale: Last-minute consumer escalations stall launches. Pre-brief surfaces the objection while it's still negotiable.

rule.statement: Rollback artefacts (feature flag, migration reverse, prompt registry pin) are tested before the gate, not after the rollout.

rationale.source: progressive delivery / Adam Zimman, LaunchDarkly Progressive Delivery

rule.rationale: Untested rollback is theater. The first real rollback is the worst time to discover it.

example.title: GA gate with pre-registered criteria
example.code: Feature: agent-X v1.0 GA Gate criteria (pre-registered 2026-04-10): - eval pass-rate ≥0.85 on golden set v3 - p95 latency ≤2.5s - cost per call ≤$0.03 - rollback tested in staging Result: 3/4 pass, latency 3.1s → defer GA
example.note: Pre-registered criteria force evidence. Result routes the decision.

example.title: Soft gate
example.code: Feature: agent-X — looks good, ship it
example.note: Political path. No evidence trail. First production incident is unexplainable.
